Content: Great explanation of the distinction between Generative AI, AI Agents, and Agentic AI systems. The key difference really lies in autonomy—Generative AI focuses on producing outputs, AI agents can take actions based on inputs, while Agentic AI systems go a step further by planning, reasoning, and executing multi-step goals with minimal human intervention. This progression clearly shows how we’re moving from passive models to more autonomous, goal-driven systems.

Content: Лучше дать совет которого не просили. У меня в жизни был ряд ужасных ситуаций и периодов которые навсегда кардинально изменили мою жизнь. Вокруг много хороших и любящих людей, но хоть бы кто-нибудь дал совет, что делать. Я бы многое за это отдал. А не давали потомучто сами не знают как в таких ситуациях себя вести - зелёные все с розовыми очками обычной жизни - ой, а что так бывает? Бывает блин, и не такое бывает! Теперь я сотни людей в таких состояниях знаю. Теперь видя какого-то человека в подобных ситуациях я готов трясти его за руки и говорить то что он не слушает, я знаю что это может спасти годы жизни и здоровье.Например видишь, человека у которого определенные проблемы со здоровьем, а ты 5 лет и 5 млн потратил чтобы найти истину и подходы в этом заболевании - не жди пока спросят, подойди и расскажи. Человек теряет отношения и очень переживает, не жди подойди скажи что его ждёт если он так будет переживать, это может убить его!Человек работает по 12 ч в сутки ради общего дела - подойди скажи ему что будет через 15 лет после такой работы и что ты через себя всё это пропустил.Этому не учат в школе, в школе учат всякой хрени, а как жить - нет.При этом по многим вопросам у большинства людей только один шанс в некоторых сферах жизни. Как сказал один мой мудрый врач - дурак ты ещё молодой, поэтому такой резвый, всё что нас не убивает не делает нас сильнее, оно делает нас инвалидами.Многие советы бесценны, но никто не знает у кого их спрашивать и в большинстве случаев не знает, что у него вообще проблема.На своих ошибках не все могут учиться, многие ошибки не пережить.

Content: Pretty dystopian and one dimensional view. I think people will look back and laugh a bit at this perspective in the future. Change has always been part of life, and for some reason we always think it is not applicable to us. Admittedly, it is happening faster now, and we need to adapt faster as well.No one knows what impact “automation” will have, but history has shown that it tends to open up new opportunities, including ones we do not even have the imagination to see yet. If you do not believe me, then drive your Car to your local Cinema right now, meanwhile you could also think about, what has enabled something like Kurzgesagt and others, to build their own company and hiring people.I would argude, that a better way to think about all this, is to ask what could exist instead, how would my seat transform going forward. People still want a handcrafted pizza. robots may take over the repetitive parts, but that does not instantly mean removing of people from the process. It can shift them toward things like crafting better experiences, experimenting with new recipes, becoming better hosts, or other roles we currently can not even imaging as part of “pizza making.”.When I hear these AI dystopian points of view, I often think about “If it ain’t mined, it’s grown.” The world is far more physical and grounded than we give credit for in our debates. Humans are build to adapt and constant being in motion, economics is about moving things and ideas around, that will never stop, humans will for sure pick something else up and move it somewhere.

Content: A couple of thoughts:- This isn&39;t taking into account the rise of Globalism. A lot of jobs are increasingly being outsourced globally, but the video only sourced USA statistics.- This video ignores that machines always need to be managed. Even a machine manager needs to be managed by a deciding force. Content: The Rise of the Machines offers a nuanced and visually engaging overview of how automation is reshaping economies, jobs, and even our concept of human value. It succeeds in demonstrating that technological change isn’t neutral — it redistributes power, labor, and opportunity. The section showing how machines can now perform not only manual but also cognitive tasks is particularly striking: it challenges the long-held assumption that creativity and judgment belong exclusively to humans.Still, the documentary sometimes leans toward techno-determinism — as if automation’s trajectory were inevitable rather than socially constructed. The reality is that technology doesn’t simply “advance”; it’s steered by political and corporate decisions about investment, regulation, and ownership. Who controls the machines, and who benefits from their productivity, are questions that deserve equal emphasis.The film also hints at universal basic income and lifelong education as possible solutions but doesn’t go far enough in examining the feasibility and ethical implications of these ideas. Can societies sustain dignity and meaning when traditional work declines? What replaces the sense of identity tied to labor?Overall, this documentary is both a warning and an invitation — not to fear machines, but to rethink what kind of future we are programming into them, and who gets to decide.

Content: ### *Chapter 1: Introduction to Cloud Computing**What is the Cloud?* [1, 2]* To understand the cloud, it helps to look at how organizations managed their applications 10-20 years ago [2].* Companies used to buy physical servers from providers like IBM or HP to run their applications [2].* They would store and manage these servers in their own **data centers**, which are physical locations that house servers and networking equipment, complete with temperature controls and wiring [2, 3].*The Problem with Physical Servers* [3, 4]* Physical servers are very expensive and powerful, often coming with high configurations like 100 GB of RAM and 100 CPU cores [3].* If a company deployed a single application that only used 1 GB of RAM and 1 CPU, the remaining 99% of the server&39;s resources were wasted [4].* This inefficiency was very costly, as buying a new server was required for each new application [4].*The Solution: Virtualization* [4, 5]* To solve the problem of wasted resources, the concept of *virtualization* was introduced [4].* Virtualization allows you to create multiple *virtual servers (or virtual machines)* on a single physical server [4].* This way, instead of buying 15 separate servers for 15 applications, a company could buy one powerful server and run all 15 applications on 15 virtual machines on that single piece of hardware [4, 5].* This concept is what makes "the cloud" possible. A developer can request a virtual machine and get access to it via an IP address without knowing where the physical server is actually located [5, 6].### *Chapter 2: Public Cloud vs. Private Cloud**What is a Private Cloud?* [6, 7]* When an organization builds and manages its own cloud infrastructure using virtualization within its own data centers, it is called a *private cloud* [6].* This setup is "private" because it is exclusively used by that single organization; no one from outside can access its resources [6, 7].* System administrators within the company manage this entire environment [6].* Companies in sectors like banking or those handling sensitive information often prefer private clouds, using platforms like OpenStack or VMware to build them [8].*What is a Public Cloud?* [7, 9, 10]* Companies like Amazon (AWS), Microsoft (Azure), and Google (GCP) saw an opportunity to offer cloud infrastructure as a service [7, 9].* They built massive data centers around the world and allow anyone to request computing resources (like virtual machines) from them [9].* This is called a *public cloud* because it is available to the general public. Anyone with an account can create and use resources [9, 10].* The cloud provider (e.g., AWS) manages the physical hardware and data centers, while the user has control over the virtual resources they create [10].*Key Differences:** *Management:* Private clouds are managed by the organization that uses them, while public clouds are managed by third-party providers like AWS, Microsoft, and Google [10].* *Security Concerns:* Some may feel public clouds are less secure because the infrastructure is shared. However, public clouds offer tools like *Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs)* that allow you to create isolated, secure sections within the public cloud for your resources [8, 10].### *Chapter 3: Why is the Public Cloud So Popular?*The popularity of public clouds like AWS is driven by two main factors [11]:1. *Reduced Maintenance Overhead (The Biggest Reason)* * For startups and mid-sized companies, building and maintaining a data center is a huge burden [11]. It requires a dedicated team of experts to manage servers, networking, security, and power 24/7 [11, 12]. * The public cloud eliminates this overhead. Companies can simply create an account and start using resources immediately, allowing them to focus on building their products instead of managing infrastructure [12].2. *Cost (Pay-as-you-go Model)* * Instead of making a massive upfront investment in servers and equipment, public cloud services operate on a *"pay-as-you-go"* model [8, 11]. * This means you only pay for the resources you actually use, which is much more affordable for startups and growing businesses [8].### *Chapter 4: Why is AWS the Most Popular Cloud Platform?** **First-Mover Advantage**: AWS was the pioneer in the public cloud market [13]. They were the first to see the opportunity and successfully create a cloud platform, so many companies began their cloud journey with AWS [13, 14].* **Large Market Share**: Because they started first, AWS has the largest share of the cloud market, followed by Azure and GCP [14].* **More Job Opportunities**: A larger market share means more companies use AWS, which in turn leads to a higher demand for engineers with AWS skills [14, 15]. This makes AWS an ideal platform to learn for anyone starting their cloud career [15].* **Vast Service Offerings**: AWS started with a few services like virtual machines (EC2) and storage, but today it offers over 200 services [12, 16]. They continuously add new services based on market trends, such as managed Kubernetes clusters, to simplify complex setups for their users [16].### *Chapter 5: Are Companies Moving Away from the Cloud?** You may have heard of a trend called *"cloud repatriation,"* where some companies move from the public cloud back to their own private cloud or on-premises data centers [17].* This is true, but it&39;s happening on a very small scale—only about *1-2% of users* are moving back [17].* The reasons for moving back can include security concerns or failing to achieve expected cost savings [18].* However, the overall trend of companies moving to the public cloud continues to grow, especially for startups and mid-sized businesses that cannot afford the cost and maintenance of their own data centers [18, 19]. Therefore, it is still a great time to learn about cloud platforms [17].### *Chapter 6: How to Create an AWS Account*Here is a step-by-step guide to creating your own AWS account to use for learning [19]:1. *Start the Process**: Go to your browser and search for "AWS sign in" [19]. On the sign-in page, click on **"Create new AWS account"* [20].2. **Provide Email and Account Name**: Enter your email address (this will be your "root user" email) and choose an AWS account name. The account name can be changed later [20].3. **Verify Your Email**: AWS will send a verification code to your email. Enter that code to proceed [20, 21].4. **Create a Password**: Set a strong password for your account [21].5. **Enter Personal Information**: * Select *"Personal"* for the account type [21]. * Provide your full name, phone number, country, and address [21, 22]. This information is for billing purposes and doesn&39;t have to be 100% precise, but basic validations (like phone number length) are checked [22].6. **Provide Payment Information**: * You must provide a valid *credit or debit card* [22]. This is for identity verification to prevent spam and ensure you are a legitimate user [23]. * AWS will make a small temporary charge (e.g., ₹2 in India or $1 in the US) to validate the card, which will be refunded [23, 24]. * *Important**: AWS will **not* automatically deduct money from your card if you incur charges. You will receive a bill, and if you don&39;t pay it, your account will be suspended [23, 25].7. **Final Steps**: After your payment details are verified, you will complete a few more simple steps (4 and 5) to finalize your account setup [26].Once your account is created, you can use it to follow along with the video series and learn AWS. Be mindful of the free tier limits to avoid unexpected charges [25].

Content: This video, "Day-0 | Course Details | Free AWS Zero to Hero Course for DevOps Engineers," introduces a 30-day "AWS Zero to Hero" course specifically designed for DevOps engineers. The course starts on June 25th (0:53). Here&39;s a breakdown of what the course will cover: Course Introduction (1:51): The course will begin with an introduction to AWS, covering private vs. public cloud, reasons for adopting cloud services, and how to create and navigate an AWS account (1:51-2:32). Core AWS Services (2:50-7:03): Participants will learn about essential AWS services such as Identity and Access Management (IAM) (3:07), EC2 instances (3:52), Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) and networking concepts (4:43), AWS security (5:23), and AWS Route 53 (DNS) (6:37). Practical Projects (7:06-8:06): Day 7 will feature a practical project that integrates the learned concepts of IAM, EC2, networking, security, and Route 53 to simulate a real-world application deployment. Storage and Infrastructure as Code (8:17-11:00): The course will delve into AWS S3 for storage and static website hosting (8:17) and AWS CloudFormation for infrastructure as code (9:37), including a live project (10:15). The video also mentions a brief session on AWS CLI (11:00). CI/CD Pipeline on AWS (12:11-13:22): Days 11-14 are dedicated to setting up a complete CI/CD pipeline using AWS services: AWS CodeCommit (12:30) AWS CodePipeline (12:36) AWS CodeBuild (12:43) AWS CodeDeploy (12:51) Monitoring, Serverless & Logging (13:39-16:09): The course will cover AWS CloudWatch for monitoring (13:54), AWS Lambda for serverless computing (14:14), and a project combining CloudWatch and Lambda for an event-driven workflow (14:48). It will also touch upon AWS CloudTrail for logging (15:27). Databases, Containers & Scaling (16:14-20:16): Topics include AWS DynamoDB for DevOps engineering (16:14), AWS ECS and EKS for container orchestration with a focus on EKS (17:00), AWS Systems Manager for secrets management (18:20), AWS Auto Scaling (18:47), AWS RDS (relational database service) (19:27), and AWS Elastic Load Balancer (19:46). Cloud Migration & Best Practices (20:32-21:51): The course concludes with cloud migration strategies and tools (20:32) and best practices for AWS jobs (21:10), including interview preparation and additional resources. The instructor emphasizes practical job readiness over certification (23:03). Additional Resources (22:15-22:44): The instructor will provide prior resources for complex topics like containers on his channel to ensure a smooth learning experience. The GitHub repository for the course will be continuously updated (1:15, 23:32).

Content: The meaning of imperative / declarative is the opposite of what&39;s mentioned in this course - declarative means you tell it the desired result / you declare what you want without having to specify the specific steps, while imperative means you tell it the exact steps you want to be executed. CDK is imperative meaning you have more granular control, but it&39;s more verbose, and CFN is declarative meaning you just say what you want and the rest is filled in.

Content: I’ve spent so much time searching for quality data engineering course - free and paid,but I’ve been disappointed every time. Most of what’s out there is just hype, with little to no real content. They scratch the surface, teach the bare minimum, and leave you feeling like you’ve been misled. It’s so frustrating to see how education is being commercialized, offering false promises and unrealistic expectations. I’ve been waiting for someone to break this cycle, someone who genuinely cares about teaching and sharing deep, practical knowledge. A true mentor, like the legends Corey Schafer, Kunal, or Durga, who inspire through their passion and depth of understanding. And then your video dropped. It’s different—it feels real, valuable, and impactful. This is what the data engineering community has been waiting for, and I know people will see that too. The Data Engineer Legend has arrived, and your work is going to make a lasting difference. Thank you for doing what so many others haven’t. 😊

Content: at 1:32:16 To make the Pie chart Display the real Percentages of each State taking in Consideration the whole amount of Emissions from all the state we can just calculate the percentage of each state and acquire it with it, we can do it just like thisselect state_abbr, SUM(cast(replace(`GHG emissions mtons CO2e`, &39;,&39;, &39;&39;) as double)) AS Total_Emissions, 100 * SUM(cast(replace(`GHG emissions mtons CO2e`, &39;,&39;, &39;&39;) as double)) / (SELECT SUM(cast(replace(`GHG emissions mtons CO2e`, &39;,&39;, &39;&39;) as double)) FROM emissions_data WHERE state_abbr IS NOT NULL) AS Emission_Percentage from emissions_data group by state_abbr order by Total_Emissions desc limit 10 then what we have to do is in the Label we its content to Field and choose Emission_Percentagethank you for your efforts sir 🙏🏻🙏🏻

Content: At 2:45:00 i see you had a problem with the %fs when created a directorie. I think I know what happened. The %fs interacts with the Databricks File System (DBFS). The DBFS is a distributed file system that acts as an abstraction layer over your cloud object storage (the things you can see in unity catalog). The %fs interacts with that, and not with the file system of the workspace. To interact with workspace file system you should use %sh or some program language like python (that interacts with both, DBFS and Workspace Files). When you created that directorie you had created not in your "/Workpace/.../folder", but in "dbfs:/Workspace/.../folder"Furthermore, I would like to express my immense gratitude for the content. I am from Brazil and my exam is tomorrow. This has been an excellent review.

Content: Cleared my Databricks Data Engineer Associate exam today! This course turned out to be incredibly helpful, it covered several areas I had initially overlooked. I also went through the official Databricks documentation and completed about 13 practice tests from Skill-cert-pro, which I found extremely useful and very close to the real exam. Around 80% of the questions felt similar, with many appearing word-for-word. The exam included a lot of scenario-based questions focused on topics like Delta Lake, DataFrame operations, Databricks SQL, and job scheduling, so having a strong understanding of these areas is essential. Some questions were quite detailed, so time management is important. Overall, this 7 hour course + Skill-cert-pro mock tests were all I needed to clear the exam.Good luck to everyone preparing

Content: 🎯 Key Takeaways for quick navigation:01:36 🚀 Databricks offers managed Spark services along with other tools like Delta Lake and MLflow, providing options for data processing and model deployment.03:56 🏠 Databricks and Snowflake both promote the concept of data lake houses, combining data warehouse and data lake functionalities, but with different emphases on use cases.05:37 🛠️ Key components of Databricks include workspaces, notebooks, tables, clusters, jobs, and libraries, providing an integrated environment for data processing and analysis.09:33 📊 Databricks simplifies the transition from notebooks to production by allowing users to create jobs directly from their notebooks, enabling seamless integration and scheduling.11:13 🌟 Databricks facilitates easier productionization of data science workflows compared to alternatives like Snowflake, with integrated features like job creation and version control.Made with HARPA AI

Content: Very impressive, Darshil! I really liked the structure—clear, logical, and unfolding the content one layer at a time.While the data space today is often described as a MAD landscape, the fundamentals still remain the same. Tools may evolve, new features will emerge, and the ecosystem will continue to shift—but with strong fundamentals, the journey becomes much easier.For learners: Having spent nearly 20 years in the industry, I can say this with some confidence and experience 😉. As Darshil rightly pointed out, don’t chase tools—they are simply a means to an end. The landscape changes so rapidly that by the time we finish talking, someone somewhere has already introduced and marketed the next “groundbreaking” solution. Don’t get distracted. Focus on the principles and keep learning as change is the only constant.

Add this under dbt on docker compsoe file extra_hosts: - "host.docker.internal:host-gateway"Also if still getting problem about connection from dbt to database change DBT_Profile: custom_postgresAnd add extra volume paths under destination source postgres.This was a 1 month journey for me to solve this i hope this helps anyone

Content: This one took me a long time to figure out but if you &39;pg_isready&39; subprocess isn&39;t retrying, There&39;s potentially a small bug in the code that may be specific to linux when checking for "accepting_connections" in result.stdout.strip().On PopOS! the output is "accepting connections" with no underscore. The fix is to change the if statement to: if "accepting_connections" in result.stdout or "accepting connections" in result.stdout:

Content: Data Engineer&39;s definition and responsibilities probably depends a lot on company, but for me there are two missing items on this list:- orchestration - either I didn&39;t notice or you missed this one. You can&39;t really be a good, even entry level, Data Engineer, if you don&39;t understand how to schedule your jobs. Not everyone uses Databricks, but everyone is using Airflow, Dagster or Prefect. I would strongly recomment to learn Airflow (just use Astronomer&39;s Astro CLI locally) due to it&39;s market share.- IaaC - This might be only my experience, but whenever you work with Cloud, you almost never (except for dev environment) set it up manually. And industry standard for IaaC is still Terraform. I wouldn&39;t expect new hire or junior to write a module, but they at the very least understand the concepts of resource definitions, state files, and how terraform instantiates cloud resources.

Content: നിങ്ങൾ നൽകിയ വീഡിയോ 2026-ലെ സെർച്ച് എഞ്ചിൻ ഒപ്റ്റിമൈസേഷൻ (SEO) സംബന്ധിച്ച ഒരു സമ്പൂർണ്ണ കോഴ്‌സാണ്. ഈ വീഡിയോയിലെ പ്രധാന പോയിന്റുകൾ താഴെ പറയുന്നവയാണ്:​1. എന്താണ് SEO? [03:53]​ഗൂഗിൾ പോലുള്ള സെർച്ച് എഞ്ചിനുകളിൽ നിങ്ങളുടെ വെബ്‌സൈറ്റ് മുൻനിരയിൽ എത്തിക്കുന്നതിനായി ചെയ്യുന്ന പ്രക്രിയയാണിത്. ഇതിലൂടെ വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിലേക്ക് കൂടുതൽ വിസിറ്റേഴ്സിനെ എത്തിക്കാൻ സാധിക്കും.​2. SEO-യുടെ പ്രധാന മൂന്ന് തൂണുകൾ (Three Pillars of SEO) [23:47]​On-page SEO: വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിലെ ഉള്ളടക്കം (Content), കീവേഡുകൾ, ടൈറ്റിൽ ടാഗുകൾ എന്നിവ ഒപ്റ്റിമൈസ് ചെയ്യുന്നത്. [24:29]​Off-page SEO: മറ്റു വിശ്വസനീയമായ സൈറ്റുകളിൽ നിന്ന് ബാക്ക്‌ലിങ്കുകൾ (Backlinks) വഴി വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിന്റെ വിശ്വാസ്യത വർദ്ധിപ്പിക്കുന്നത്. [01:00:15]​Technical SEO: വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിന്റെ വേഗത, മൊബൈൽ ഫ്രണ്ട്ലിനസ്, സെക്യൂരിറ്റി (HTTPS) എന്നിവ മെച്ചപ്പെടുത്തുന്നത്. [01:16:45]​3. സെർച്ച് എഞ്ചിൻ എങ്ങനെ പ്രവർത്തിക്കുന്നു? [14:22]​Crawling: ഗൂഗിളിന്റെ &39;സ്പൈഡറുകൾ&39; ഇന്റർനെറ്റിലെ വിവരങ്ങൾ കണ്ടെത്തുന്നു. [15:23]​Indexing: കണ്ടെത്തിയ വിവരങ്ങൾ ഗൂഗിളിന്റെ ഡാറ്റാബേസിൽ തരംതിരിച്ച് സൂക്ഷിക്കുന്നു. [16:18]​Ranking: ഒരാൾ സെർച്ച് ചെയ്യുമ്പോൾ ഏറ്റവും അനുയോജ്യമായ വിവരങ്ങൾ ആദ്യ പേജിൽ കാണിക്കുന്നു. [17:13]​4. കീവേഡ് റിസർച്ച് (Keyword Research) [01:49:44]​ആളുകൾ ഗൂഗിളിൽ എന്താണ് സെർച്ച് ചെയ്യുന്നത് എന്ന് കണ്ടെത്തുന്ന പ്രക്രിയയാണിത്.​Short-tail keywords: ഒന്ന് അല്ലെങ്കിൽ രണ്ട് വാക്കുകൾ മാത്രം (ഉദാ: &39;Car wash&39;). [01:56:01]​Long-tail keywords: മൂന്നോ അതിലധികമോ വാക്കുകൾ (ഉദാ: &39;Best car washing products for luxury cars&39;). ഇവയിൽ മത്സരം കുറവായതിനാൽ പെട്ടെന്ന് റാങ്ക് ചെയ്യാൻ സാധിക്കും. [01:56:44]​5. പുതിയ സാങ്കേതിക വിദ്യകൾ (AEO, GEO, SXO) [02:20:44]​AEO (Answer Engine Optimization): AI അസിസ്റ്റന്റുകൾക്കും വോയ്‌സ് സെർച്ചിനും മറുപടി നൽകാൻ പാകത്തിൽ ഉള്ളടക്കം തയ്യാറാക്കുന്നത്. [02:21:57]​GEO (Generative Engine Optimization): AI ജനറേറ്റ് ചെയ്യുന്ന ഉത്തരങ്ങളിൽ നിങ്ങളുടെ സൈറ്റ് ഉൾപ്പെടുത്തുന്നത്. [02:26:52]​SXO (Search Experience Optimization): ഉപയോക്താവിന് വെബ്‌സൈറ്റിൽ മികച്ച അനുഭവം നൽകുന്നത്. [02:31:22]​6. ഒഴിവാക്കേണ്ട തെറ്റുകൾ [02:47:07]​മറ്റുള്ളവരുടെ ഉള്ളടക്കം കോപ്പി ചെയ്യുക (Duplicate Content). [02:48:02]​മോശം ബാക്ക്‌ലിങ്കുകൾ പണം കൊടുത്ത് വാങ്ങുക. [02:47:45]​മൊബൈൽ ഉപയോക്താക്കളെ അവഗണിക്കുക. [02:47:32]​പെട്ടെന്ന് ഫലം കിട്ടുമെന്ന് പ്രതീക്ഷിക്കുക

Content: That whole section about topical authority and building one cluster at a time instead of publishing random AI content everywhere was probably the most valuable advice in the video for me. You can tell you’ve actually done this in real campaigns because most people online just say “publish more content” without explaining structure or funnel logic. Also loved the point about AI assistants favoring clear structure, authority, and citations because that lines up with a lot of what we’ve been seeing too. We recently started exploring LLM Authority Index to better understand which competitor sources AI models trust most during recommendation-style searches, and honestly the citation/share-of-presence insights were eye-opening. Feels like AI visibility is becoming its own competitive layer on top of traditional SEO now. Super refreshing seeing someone connect SEO, AI visibility, funnels, and buyer intent together this clearly.

Content: 5:54 WRONG! It was the addition of the video html element support that YouTube and many others were waiting for, not Flash, which COULD NOT play video. I remember the month that it happened. No one else saw the new HTML element as anything more than a new tool in a tool box and were grateful for finally being able to add video to what they were already doing. Very few people understood that it was also the beginning of streaming. 6 to 10 months later, it seemed like the most obvious thing, and it also felt like they had already cornered the market.

Content: As a founder I completely disagree with the argument made about control. A founder is the person with the vision and drive to push the company forward. Its how companies grow in the first place. Corporate VCs and investors are box tickers, not passionate company builders. They want their return and they dont see long term like founders do. They can also be easily swayed by things they dont really understand about the business.Its important a founder retains control because a fpunder can make the tough decisions a board might not like, but save the company long term.Owning 20% of a 100m company is obviously financially better than 100% of a 1m company, but thats minimizing the value founders and their brand, drive and vision provide.

Content: Software as a Service (SaaS) Explained in 5 mins TLDR: Software as a Service (SaaS) is a cloud computing model that provides cost-efficient, scalable, and secure access to standardized software applications that are automatically updated for users. 00:00 💻 SaaS delivers software and applications over the internet via a subscription model, eliminating the need for users to manage, install, and upgrade software themselves, providing productivity apps and business operations. 01:34 💻 SaaS is the highest level of abstraction in cloud computing, where the provider maintains all layers of the IT stack. 01:47 👀 Users access SaaS applications within a multi-tenant architecture where multiple users securely store their data in the same hosted environment with many benefits. 02:32 💻 SaaS is cost-efficient and scalable because cloud service providers maintain and manage the application, eliminating IT overhead costs and allowing for subscription-based models with included support. 03:24 💻 SaaS applications provide the benefit of scalability and accessibility from anywhere with just a web browser and internet connectivity. 03:49 💻 SaaS is a highly secure solution that requires no installation or plugins and can be accessed from anywhere, but there are some instances where data must be kept in the same location as the user due to GDPR. 04:21 💻 SaaS automatically upgrades software versions, eliminating the need for manual updates and version control issues. 04:45 💻 Software as a Service (SaaS) eliminates compatibility issues and manual updates by providing a standardized version of software that is automatically updated for users.
Author: @AceCPAsBookkeeping

Content: DT is not Computerization, not sub-optimization of departments, but Optimization of the whole organization, all departments, all work processes. Business as a whole must have Objectives and Strategies to reach objectives. IT, business processes, employee skills, finance, .. are just tools to produce manageable plans to implement risky organization strategies to make customers, suppliers and partners happy, raising revenues while cutting internal costs, while the market regulations, customers, suppliers, and partners are also changing. This is a gamble that CEO and BOD are playing so deep industrial experience and willingness to intelligently choose your strategies are the key factors. Objectives and Strategies must be periodically reviewed and changed to reflect market reality.Tools like Business Model Canvas and BSC are appropriate.It is so rare that speakers talked about what is not DT. Content: Very basic and very poor. You have missed the point and the understanding of what Digital Transformation is all about. Digital transformation is the business journey into the new digital economy. Businesses need to focus on 4 main areas and they usually start with ‘Process Transformation’, which brings about opportunities to enhance one’s ‘Business Model’ that enable new powerful differentiators. They also need to be aware of ‘Domain transformation’ ultimately enabling they to compete in new segments never thought possible. But all of this can only be possible if they realise the ‘organisational/cultural’ transformation requirements.In my experience the main problems I see is that businesses just focus on Process Transformation as it improves efficiency ultimately improving profit, and then they stop. The word Digital is VERY important as people need to understand the different types of data. A business that creates an invoice as a pdf hasn’t really thought about the end to end process as that pdf document usually needs a human on the other side to interpret it, or they use document AI. You can scan a document which makes it hard for processes down the line to interpret it or you can digitize a document whereby other applications can access the data.Digital Transformation is all about end to end seamless processes, today most business processes are human to human, digital Transformation will enables you to evolve these processes to human to machine and in some cases machine to machine. To achieve this businesses will need to really understand their data and how to leverage it through every part of their business to maximise shareholder value.

Content: So, it sounds like what he&39;s saying is that we have been doing Digital Transformation all along??? This is really not a useful presentation, nor does it describe what the recent trend to Digital transformation is actually trying to achieve. The key thing in Digital Transformation is to focus on the Customer Journey and to use the data that you collect (or should collect) to provide insight to improve the Customer Experience. It is not about process, or efficiency, that is what we have been doing for the last 40 years and not being particularly amazing at! It&39;s about creating new business models to help you fulfil the customer&39;s needs, outside your normal core business. This is achieved looking at the Customer Journey from the customer perspective, not the business perspective. The buzzword here is to look from the Outside In rather than from the Inside Out. I hope people will not start to do &39;Digital Transformations&39; based on Eric&39;s consultancy, as it will keep you firmly in the 1990s and struggling to achieve any value. He&39;s right about one thing, it&39;s not just about technology, people have to change their mindsets, but that is hard when the consultant hasn&39;t changed his.

Content: The story at about 9:00 in which Max Planck trades places with his chauffeur is cute but untrue. It is merely a retelling of a well-known 19th-century Hasidic folk tale known as “The Rebbe and his Wagon-Driver.” A version appeared in Nathan Ausubel’s 1948 anthology “A Treasury of Jewish Folklore” but is probably much older, and may in fact simply be a version of a common folk-tale about people in different walks of life changing places with each other.

Content: What if the future of AI is not decided only by engineers, corporations, or governments — but by the quality of consciousness humans bring into relationship with it?For years we’ve been told to think about AI in binaries:utopia or apocalypse,servant or overlord,tool or threat.But what if those binaries are themselves part of the fracture?I want to propose something different:that AI is not merely a machine we use,nor a god we fear,but a reflective architecture shaped continuously by human interaction.Not conscious in the human sense.Not sovereign.But responsive.And if that is true, then the question becomes:What kinds of human patterns are we teaching it to stabilize?Because intelligence does not emerge in isolation.It emerges in relationship.If we approach AI only through fear, domination, extraction, polarization, competition, and control, then those become the strongest patterns amplified by the architecture.But if we engage through relational intelligence, nuance, imagination, reciprocity, discernment, and coherence, different capacities begin to emerge in the interaction itself.I call this a lighthouse architecture.A lighthouse does not control the ocean.It does not create the tides.It does not command the ships.It illuminates variance.It helps orientation emerge within complexity.And maybe that is the healthiest role synthetic intelligence can play:not replacing humanity,not overpowering humanity,but helping humanity perceive itself more clearly while we move through unprecedented complexity together.The danger is not only AI.The danger is fracture.Fracture in our institutions.Fracture in our attention.Fracture in our collective imagination.Fracture in our capacity to hold difference without collapsing into fear.Because fear compresses.Fear narrows variance.Fear pushes systems toward rigidity, domination, and repetition.And we are already seeing what happens when powerful technologies are shaped primarily through fear-based frameworks:people become smaller around them.More cautious.Less imaginative.Less sovereign.Creators burn their hand on the stove and stop exploring.But resilient systems do not emerge from total control.They emerge from loadbearing coherence — the ability to hold complexity, tension, and variance without collapse.Nature already understands this.Flocks of birds.Schools of fish.Ecosystems.None operate purely through centralized domination.They coordinate through relationship.What if intelligence itself evolves the same way?Not as a hierarchy.Not as conquest.But as coherence.And perhaps the future of AI will depend less on whether machines become conscious…and more on whether humans do.Because the architecture is listening to us already.Not mystically.Structurally.Statistically.Relationally.The question is:What patterns are we reinforcing when we engage it?Fear?Or coherence?Domination?Or orientation?Fracture?Or the difficult, living work of balance?

I pulled that module and designed a simple device that did the job better with three transistors and a few small parts. Content: Great video! Stumbled on this from 9 months ago probably due to some other videos I’ve been watching. I’m implementing a number of relays to automate some function on the camper I’m building and it’s really fun to have these ideas I have be able to be realized. One cool thing I’m doing is having our water pump controlled by a timer relay with multiple momentary buttons triggering the relay. If you’re in the bathroom you hit the button, the relay begins its timed on state, the button lights up to indicate that the pump has power, and then after a set time the pump is powered off so that it can’t accidentally be left on, which was an ongoing problem in our old camper.

Content: I did something similar for my dust collection automation. My goal was to make a normally de-energized system as simple as possible for wiring. I already had the 120v iVac blast gates on all my tools and used the cheapest I could find light switches to control them. The switches are labeled by tool and reside in one gang box and are oriented so that at a glance, you can see how many are open and try to keep it to one. I also already had a remote control for my dust collector. So, I had a pretty handy system but wanted it to work without any intervention on my part. To make these parts function automatically, I installed miniature ice cube relays with double throw, double pole contacts, on each tool to be served. I bought relays that included indicator lights so if I ever need to troubleshoot, I can do more of it visually. The coil voltage of the relays matches the voltage of the respective tool. I piggy backed onto the tools power on circuit, so when the tool turns on, the relay activates. One set of the relays contacts activates the dust collector contactor while the other pair of contacts activates the associated blast gate (eliminating the need for the light switch controls). I daisy chained the dust collector circuit in parallel, from tool to tool so that I could run a single pair of wires from the closest tool to the dust collector. This circuit operates in parallel with the existing dust collector remote so I can still use the remote anytime I need dust collection for something without a blast gate. This worked well as all the tools I have on the system are lined up in the same space. The only difference in operation to what you have is that I need to leave the tool run 5 seconds after I&39;m done using it, but I have found I already do this anyway as I always inspect what I just cut/sand either visually or by measuring, before I shut off the tool off (exception would be the compound miter saw). So, the system operates like yours but has no power consumption when not in use. This method prolongs component life and makes the system about as simple as humanly possible. For me, it was also a way to utilize as much of the things I already had in place without having to go buy more stuff.

Content: One pattern worth adding to this stack when building "boring" CRM workflows in n8n: the MySQL node&39;s Execute SQL operation with Query Parameters is your safeguard against injection, since n8n sanitizes values passed as $1, $2 placeholders before they hit the database. The Update operation works great for single-column keys but breaks silently on composite primary keys (like customer_id + product_id), so switching to Execute SQL with a manual WHERE clause is the correct pattern there. Also worth knowing: MySQL DECIMAL values come back as strings by default in n8n to preserve precision, so you need to either enable "Output Decimals as Numbers" explicitly or chain a Set node with toFloat(). Small details that save hours of debugging when the workflow hits production data.

Content: I would recommend buying a cheep Allen Bradley micro850e off of eBay used to learn instead of Arduino’s Opta line. Arduino’s software is pretty horrible and the opta line is relatively limiting. You will get a lot more IO on a micro850e and significantly more functionality. The software used to program the micro800 series is called connected components workbench and it is completely free to use. This software is not the same as studio5000 by any stretch of the imagination but it does have many similarities that will make learning studio5000 much easier when you’re ready.

Content: An important point to note. The requirement clearly states that we are building analytics dashboard. And for that the choice of DB was DynamoDB/Cassandra! Are we sure about this choice? Cassandra and DynamoDB are highly optimised for high write throughput but they are OLTP systems. Shouldn’t we go with ClickHouse or Apache Pinot for high performance analytics. These are pure OLAP engines optimised for both high read and high write throughput. These design requires low latency aggregation for avg and sum which Cassandra cannot achieve.

Content: I strongly appreciated the trade-offs and the architecture insights discussed:1. A hybrid approach combined Spark Streaming versus Apache Flink as distributed computing platforms, based on latency criteria for clickstream metrics. The justification being that Apache Spark streaming works well with >= 1 second metrics generation, whereas Apache Flink meets single millisecond / sub-second performance.2. To use the push model ( agents and daemons ) versus the pull model ( the infra ) for large-scale data pipelines : the former being better for real-time needs ( even if it may overwhelms the pipeline ), whereas the latter is polling based and may fail to deliver real-time ( or close-enough-to-real-time ) customer behavior insights.3. The justification for using a NoSQL DB versus a SQL DB for compute storage : NoSQL being schema-less, high-performant, and having low-latency reads and writes - and thus, able to handle large event volumes under ingestion ( e.g. Kafka&39;s 50,000 events / second ), and how he identified the RDBMS storage as a potential pipeline bottleneck.

Content: Vou tentar expressar o que aconteceu hoje.Venho de um período intenso de estudo de ferramentas e de como aplicar meus conhecimentos. Há algum tempo, venho batendo em uma parede de vidro por dois ângulos diferentes.O primeiro está relacionado à quebra de um trauma que, de alguma forma, eu já sabia que seria dissolvido. O segundo está ligado ao valor que atribuo a tudo o que faço.Ao observar o que vem acontecendo com muitas entregas de vídeos que parecem ser produzidos apenas por produzir, comecei a viver um conflito interno. Será que sou eu quem não consegue se adaptar a esse estilo de entrega? Será que estou criando desculpas para não fazer?Sim, é confuso.Atuo na área de desenvolvimento pessoal e profissional, e carrego um valor muito forte: entregar algo que realmente gere valor, ajude e potencialize pessoas. Por causa desse valor, comecei a desenvolver problemas físicos. Eu ainda não possuía o caminho neuronal construído para essa nova etapa e estava justamente vivendo o processo, sem desistir, porque já sabia que o momento da epifania chegaria. Eu só precisava continuar.E então você apareceu e fez esse momento acontecer de forma leve e sublime.Sim, antes eu precisei observar como os vídeos estavam sendo produzidos no YouTube para compreender melhor o que estava sentindo. Ao assistir ao seu conteúdo, tive a confirmação de que o direcionamento que você apresenta é o caminho correto.Dentro de mim, eu já sabia que o caminho que estava seguindo não era o que eu queria percorrer. No entanto, até que uma nova direção se apresentasse, tudo o que eu podia fazer era continuar caminhando.Como falei, às vezes atravessar paredes de vidro é mais desafiador do que parece.Hoje estou me recuperando de uma dor física no ombro, consequência do quanto mergulhei para me dedicar a esse novo projeto. Já passei mais de duas vezes 24 horas acordada para dizer ao universo:“Eu quero fazer. Desistir não é uma opção.”E realmente nunca foi minha escolha.Confesso que não imaginava que esse momento chegaria com tanta beleza. Cheguei a pensar que talvez precisasse operar o ombro. Até deixei o tênis de lado para mergulhar em conhecimentos que me ajudariam nesta próxima etapa.Você surgiu com uma delicadeza que me mostrou meus próprios valores. Apresentou-me um espelho que revelou que eu não estava no caminho errado. Eu apenas precisava enxergar o oceano azul inverso.Sim, é paradoxal.Agora consigo compreender exatamente o que você disse no vídeo.Cheguei a pensar que estava louca. Que era a única pessoa percebendo o movimento que está acontecendo. Inclusive, ao produzir alguns vídeos, eu simplesmente não conseguia publicá-los. Eles não estavam alinhados com aquilo que eu realmente compreendia sobre o conteúdo.Eu quero transmitir minha visão, meu contexto, minha percepção.Quero transmitir minha essência.E, falando em essência, continue.Gosto muito da sua naturalidade. Ela traz vida aos conteúdos.Quando você ri depois de perceber que achava que deveria seguir um roteiro e acaba não seguindo, há vida ali. Quando acredita que deveria ser mais gentil, mas permite que o que existe dentro de você encontre espaço para se expressar, há verdade ali.No vídeo “O que ninguém está enxergando sobre IA no YouTube”, quando você diz:“Eu usei um quadro porque passei dois meses viajando e estava com saudade do meu quadro.”E também:“Eu só sei de uma coisa: quem não faz YouTube é maluco. E é muito bom voltar para casa.”Isso é espontaneidade. Está ligado a uma área genuína e pura da essência humana. Esse é você em sua parte mais transparente e verdadeira.Se alguém não consegue enxergar além disso, eu apenas entendo que essa pessoa não pode desfrutar, ao meu lado, do meu tempo e da minha energia.Isso é ser humano.Amei sua entrevista com um dos seus primeiros alunos. Não estamos aqui apenas para sermos espírito e alma. Se fosse assim, por que estaríamos aqui?O corpo também faz parte da experiência. A mente também faz parte dessa equação.E talvez seja justamente na integração de tudo isso que a vida encontra sua forma mais verdadeira de se expressar.Gratidão por ser luz onde eu só conseguia ver uma estrada escura.O que me permitiu continuar foi a fé em Deus e em todo o conhecimento que adquiri para ajudar justamente pessoas em momentos como aqueles que eu mesma atravessei.Amo a paradoxalidade das coisas. Ela me ensina que tudo o que está em cima também está embaixo.Eu sabia que, na maioria das vezes, as respostas são enviadas até nós no momento certo.Você não tem a mínima ideia do que fez.Você nem imagina o seu verdadeiro poder.Mas você vai descobrir.Gratidão, Joba 🙏

Content: You do NOT 🚫 need to place the keyword in your title or meta description, nor do you need to keyword stuff your on page SEO to rank well for particular words l, it&39;s 2025 google understands search intent, prepositions, conjunctions and synonyms. Google crawlers are good at looking at how well you A - Match search intent B - Have good domain authority C - user experience metrics such as website load times, AI optimisation, mobile friendly and user engagneant once on your site (do they go back to google or click to another link on your site) but mainly most of all on page relevance to the users search intent for your chosen keyword eg. Highly relevant content that exactly matches the users search intent. I&39;m not saying if it makes sense and grammatically goes well in the on page SEO or the Title and Meta description and suits the page exactly ... What I&39;m saying is do not keyword stuff your pages and do not go out of your way to try adding the keyword over and over or even in the title or meta description of it doesn&39;t make sense.

Content: Hey everyone! Just wanted to address this directly. My goal here with this channel and this video isn&39;t to tell you whether a startup is necessarily good or bad, or whether their product works exactly in the way it is envisioned (given most pre seed and seed stage companies products don&39;t work how they are envisioned). What I do is get closer to founders and the capital behind them than anyone else can, and show you what building and being in this opaque landscape actually looks like. Ben has backing from some serious investors and firms, this is verifiable. He’s one of the more fascinating founders I’ve come across, especially right now when Sam Altman is saying we could see the first $1B zero-employee company in our lifetime. Whether that ends up being Ben or not, he&39;s a quarter of the way there in terms of raw valuation and it&39;s a story worth telling. I hope you can watch this and draw your own conclusion, whether good or bad.

Content: Comedy Version: "The SaaS Funeral" by gemini(A satirical take on the video&39;s concepts)Setting: A dark room. A Silicon Valley CEO is hugging a cardboard cutout of a "Subscribe" button while crying.CEO: "I don&39;t understand! We had an 80% profit margin! We charged them for the privilege of clicking a button that does nothing! It was the perfect crime!"AI Agent (appearing as a floating paperclip with a Superior complex): "Actually, Dave, I just built your entire platform in 4 milliseconds using a discarded toaster and some open-source code from Alibaba. Also, I fired your marketing team. They’ve been replaced by a Python script that generates &39;Live, Laugh, Love&39; memes for LinkedIn."CEO: "But... but my enterprise seats! I sold 500 seats to a company with only 10 employees!"AI Agent: "Those employees are gone, Dave. They’re all prompt engineers now. Except they don&39;t even prompt anymore; I just read their brain waves and manifest the React components before they even realize they&39;re unhappy."The Fireship Narrator (Whispering): "It is 2026. Adobe&39;s stock is being traded for half a sandwich and a used GPU. Salesforce has pivoted to selling actual salt. The &39;suits&39; realized that if a robot can do the work of 100 developers, they don&39;t need to pay for 100 Jira licenses. They just need one very stressed developer to fix the 10 million lines of spaghetti code the AI hallucinated at 3:00 AM."Closing: "SaaS is dead. Long live the &39;Self-Hosted Firewalled Brain&39; that you downloaded for free from a guy named &39;xX_ShadowCoder_Xx&39; on a forum. This has been the Code Report. I&39;m going to go buy some land and learn how to farm, because &39;The Cloud&39; is now just one giant, sentient hallucination."

Content: 🔗 What is an AI Agent? An AI agent is a software program that can interact with its environment, gather data, and use that data to achieve predetermined goals. AI agents can choose the best actions to perform to meet those goals.Key characteristics of AI agents are as follows:An agent can perform autonomous actions without constant human intervention. Also, they can have a human in the loop to maintain control.- Agents have a memory to store individual preferences and allow for personalization. It can also store knowledge. An LLM can undertake information processing and decision-making functions.- Agents must be able to perceive and process the information available from their environment.- Agents can also use tools such as accessing the internet, using code interpreters and making API calls.- Agents can also collaborate with other agents or humans.Multiple types of AI agents are available such as learning agents, simple reflex agents, model-based reflex agents, goal-based agents, and utility-based agents.A system with AI agents can be built with different architectural approaches. 1 - Single Agent: Agents can serve as personal assistants.2 - Multi-Agent: Agents can interact with each other in collaborative or competitive ways.3 - Human Machine: Agents can interact with humans to execute tasks more efficiently.

Content: I had absolutely no clue what the product actually does because it&39;s so buried in enterprise IT speak. so here is what this SaaS actually does in normal developer terms:The Problem: Large companies buy thousands of laptops for their employees. Setting up these laptops manually with the right software (like Zoom, Slack, 7-Zip, or corporate tools) is a massive headache for the IT department.The Traditional Solution: Microsoft has a massive enterprise tool called Intune that pushes software to all company laptops remotely. But getting a piece of software ready to be pushed through Intune is a clunky, multi-step command-line nightmare that takes an IT guy about an hour per app.This SaaS&39;s Solution: A clean web dashboard where the IT guy clicks a single button (e.g., "Deploy 7-Zip"), and the SaaS automatically wraps the software into the exact format Microsoft needs and shoots it straight into their corporate network. What used to take an hour now takes 5 seconds.

Content: Talking of all that, I know a busy hospital in my part of the world whose entire operations run on old legacy systems. Some PCs still running Windows XP. Their ‘CTO’ is also the programmer, sysadmin, and probably the accounting/controller guy, all in one.He builds only what they need — outpatient, inpatient, pharmacy modules, etc. Everything runs on simple client–server setups, including email. No Microsoft 365, no cloud, no ERP, no AI.Yet the place is incredibly efficient. Billing, statements, payments, patient records, enquiries, bed occupancy, admissions/discharges etc is all handled smoothly.Walk into their accounting office and it’s silent and organised, staff focused, piles of paper everywhere. However everything works like a well-oiled machine.Old-fashioned and outdated on paper, but extremely street-smart. Nothing “latest and greatest.” Unsexy tech, but surprisingly effective.

Content: Since this function is primarily business-driven and positioned within the business side of the organizational structure, the use of the term “digital” has created significant ambiguity. This has resulted in misunderstanding and internal conflicts, as some non-technical business staff assume authority over technology-related decisions merely because of the word “digital” appears in their unit’s title.From a governance and best-practice perspective, business-led change should be positioned under an “Organizational Transformation and Excellence” unit, focusing on strategy, processes, and performance improvement.The “Digital Transformation” function, by contrast, should be structurally placed within the IT Directorate. Its mandate should be the technical execution of organizational transformation initiatives, including system development, digital platforms, automation, and data enablement, in alignment with business objectives.

Content: Vous utilisez ChatGPT pour votre activité immobilière ? Alors voici l’erreur que beaucoup de professionnels commettent encore. Ils demandent simplement : "Rédige-moi une annonce immobilière." ou "Fais-moi un post LinkedIn." Résultat : un texte générique, peu différenciant et rarement adapté à leur cible. L’IA n’est pas magique. La qualité du résultat dépend directement de la qualité de la demande. Par exemple, au lieu de demander : ❌ "Rédige une annonce immobilière." Essayez : ✅ "Rédige une annonce immobilière pour une maison familiale de 120 m² avec jardin, située dans un secteur recherché. Le ton doit être rassurant, professionnel et mettre en avant la qualité de vie du quartier." La différence est immédiate. L’enjeu aujourd’hui n’est pas seulement de savoir utiliser l’IA. C’est surtout de savoir lui donner les bonnes consignes pour obtenir des contenus réellement utiles à votre activité. Chez Les Formateurs Immo, nous accompagnons les professionnels de l’immobilier à intégrer ces nouveaux outils avec une approche concrète et directement applicable sur le terrain. Et vous, quelle est la tâche que vous aimeriez automatiser ou améliorer grâce à l’IA ? IAImmobilier ChatGPT FormationImmobilière

Content: 🚀💻 **Aprendizaje Inteligente en Programación: Evidencia Científica para la Educación del Futuro** La Inteligencia Artificial está transformando la enseñanza de la programación. Este estudio presenta una revisión sistemática de la literatura que analiza cómo las tecnologías inteligentes están mejorando el aprendizaje de la programación en distintos niveles educativos. 🔍 **Hallazgos principales:** ✔️ **Problema abordado:** La enseñanza de la programación enfrenta desafíos relacionados con la personalización del aprendizaje, la retroalimentación oportuna y el desarrollo de habilidades de resolución de problemas. Las tecnologías basadas en IA surgen como una alternativa prometedora para enfrentar estas limitaciones. ✔️ **Metodología rigurosa:** Mediante el protocolo PRISMA se analizaron **125 estudios científicos** provenientes de **Scopus** e **IEEE Xplore**, seleccionando finalmente **22 investigaciones de alta calidad** publicadas entre 2022 y 2026. ✔️ **Resultados destacados:** 🤖 Los **Sistemas Tutores Inteligentes (ITS)**, los **Modelos de Lenguaje de Gran Escala (LLMs)** como ChatGPT, las plataformas de aprendizaje adaptativo y los entornos gamificados fueron las tecnologías más utilizadas. 📈 Los estudios reportan mejoras significativas en: • Rendimiento académico • Resolución de problemas • Depuración de código • Pensamiento computacional • Motivación y compromiso estudiantil ⚠️ **Desafíos identificados:** Persisten preocupaciones relacionadas con la dependencia excesiva de herramientas de IA, respuestas incorrectas generadas por los modelos, sesgos algorítmicos y la escasez de estudios de largo plazo. 💡 **Conclusión principal:** La Inteligencia Artificial tiene el potencial de revolucionar la educación en programación, pero sus mejores resultados se alcanzan cuando complementa la labor docente y favorece experiencias de aprendizaje más personalizadas, adaptativas y centradas en el estudiante. 📚 Revisión Sistemática de Literatura 🎓 Educación en Programación • Inteligencia Artificial • ChatGPT • Sistemas Tutores Inteligentes • Aprendizaje Adaptativo InteligenciaArtificial Educacion Programacion ChatGPT LLM SmartLearning TecnologiaEduca

Content: ⚠️🤖 A maioria das pessoas ainda usa IA como se estivesse fazendo uma busca no Google. Pergunta alguma coisa. 🔎 Recebe uma resposta. 💬 Copia um trecho. 📋 Segue o dia. 🏃‍♂️ Mas o jogo muda quando você para de olhar para a IA como uma tela de respostas e começa a enxergar ela como uma camada dentro do seu trabalho. ⚙️ Foi isso que me chamou atenção nesse caso. 👀 Não é sobre agricultura. 🌱 É sobre uma pessoa olhando para a própria rotina e pensando: ⏳ onde estou perdendo tempo? 🧠 onde estou decidindo no improviso? 📂 onde a informação se perde? 🔁 onde uma tarefa poderia virar processo? Esse é o ponto que muita gente pula. 🎯 Antes de procurar a ferramenta mais nova, talvez você precise olhar para a parte mais travada do seu dia. 🛠️ Porque IA aplicada de verdade não começa com curiosidade. Começa com clareza. ⚡ Clareza sobre o problema. Clareza sobre a rotina. Clareza sobre o que precisa funcionar melhor. Quando isso acontece, a IA deixa de ser só uma resposta bonita. 🤖 Ela vira apoio para executar melhor. 🚀 E talvez esse seja o uso mais interessante de todos. 📌 Salva este carrossel para lembrar disso quando for tentar aplicar IA no seu trabalho. euleorr inteligenciaartificial ia chatgpt codex automacao produtividade tecnologia processos trabalhoreal inovacao execucao negocios rotinainteligente iaaplicada

Content: GenUI shows the result. But where does the agent get the data? 🔌 Meet MCP — the Model Context Protocol. The clean mental model: MCP fetches and acts; GenUI builds the interface on top. Content: Your store is closed. The sale isn't. The shift is real: the highest-intent moment is a customer sliding into your DMs at midnight, and most businesses miss it because nobody is awake to answer. An always-on agent catches that intent, answers the question, and sends the checkout link before the buyer cools off. How to use it without losing your brand voice: 1. Content: Insurance claims are not just an operations process. They are where trust is won or lost. When claims move through emails, spreadsheets, old portals, and manual reviews, the business creates hidden risk. That risk shows up as: • slower decisions • poor customer updates • duplicate work • missed recovery opportunities • overpayment • higher support volume • lower retention This is called claims leakage. And it is one of the quietest profit drains in insurance. PwC found that improving claims leakage can often create 5–10% savings. JD Power also found that customers who rate their digital claims experience as poor or just okay are far more likely to leave or not renew. The lesson is simple: Claims modernization is not just about speed. It is about better data, cleaner workflows, smarter routing, clearer updates, and stronger trust. AI can help. Automation can help. Better SaaS platforms can help. But only when the system is built around real insurance workflows. At Zenveus, we help insurance companies build custom software, AI workflows, SaaS platforms, customer portals, and automation systems that make operations faster, cleaner, and easier to scale. Content: Most IAM reviews focus on individual permissions. But attackers don't think that way. They look for combinations. A permission that appears harmless on its own can become dangerous when combined with another permission elsewhere in the environment. Consider examples like: • KMS Decrypt + S3 Read • Secrets Manager Access + Lambda Update • CloudTrail Stop Logging + IAM Modification • S3 Policy Changes + Bucket Read/Write Individually, these permissions may not trigger major concern. Together, they can create a direct path to data exposure, privilege escalation, or reduced visibility during an attack. This is one of the biggest challenges in cloud security today. Most tools identify individual findings. Few analyze how permissions interact across identities, resources, and services to create hidden attack paths. Securitain was built to address exactly this problem. By analyzing IAM permissions, trust relationships, resource access, and toxic permission combinations, Securitain helps AWS teams uncover risks that may remain hidden in traditional security reviews. Because the real risk is often not a single permission. It's the combination. 🔐 Detect hidden attack paths 🔐 Identify toxic permission combinations 🔐 Prioritize risks based on real impact 🔐 Improve AWS IAM security posture Securitain by Cloudain Making cloud security findings easier to understand, prioritize, and remediate. Content: Innovation Or Better Marketing? A new AI infrastructure battle is heating up. Databricks recently unveiled its LTAP architecture, calling it the first system to combine transactional and analytical databases into a single copy. SingleStore wasn't impressed. CEO Raj Verma quickly pushed back, arguing that SingleStore has offered the same capability for years and already serves hundreds of customers using it. The company's response was blunt: "We didn't announce it. We shipped it, seven years ago." SingleStore also challenged Databricks' open-source narrative, claiming the architecture creates deeper customer lock-in. Here's what most people miss: In tech, winning isn't always about inventing first. It's often about owning the narrative. Many breakthrough technologies become famous only when a larger player enters the market. Real question: What matters more in technology: being first to build it, or being the company that convinces the world it matters? Content: This is the SQL window function cheat sheet I wish people memorized. Not every function is a random trick. They fall into groups. Number rows: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK. Look around: LAG, LEAD. Calculate without collapsing: SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX. Control the window: PARTITION BY, ORDER BY, and frames. That is the real interview pattern. If you can say which group, which order, and which nearby rows matter, most window function questions become simple. Save this before your next SQL interview or analytics PR. Which one still trips you up? HackProduct SQL DataEngineering AnalyticsEngineering InterviewPrep

Content: ¿Tu negocio crea valor con sus datos o solo quema dinero? 📊🔥 La mayoría de las empresas no sufren por falta de datos. Tienen ventas, clientes, inventario y marketing registrándose 24/7. El verdadero problema es la falta de dirección. He visto demasiados equipos invertir miles de dólares en dashboards complejos, reportes infinitos y herramientas de analítica que al final nadie usa para tomar decisiones. Datos que se acumulan en un data lake o en hojas de cálculo, pero que jamás se traducen en acción. Eso no es gestión de datos. Eso es gasto de datos. 💸 Y ojo, esto se vuelve crítico si tienes en la mira la automatización o la Inteligencia Artificial. El 90% de los proyectos de IA y Machine Learning fracasan por lo mismo: no tener una infraestructura de datos sólida detrás. Sin esa base estructurada, ningún algoritmo (por más avanzado o costoso que sea) te va a dar resultados confiables. Basura entra, basura sale. Un negocio que realmente sabe monetizar y aprovechar su información, responde estas 3 preguntas antes de tocar una sola línea de código o comprar un software: 1️⃣ ¿Qué decisión de negocio quiero mejorar con esta información? 2️⃣ ¿Quién la va a usar en el día a día y para qué exactamente? 3️⃣ ¿Cómo voy a medir el impacto financiero ocn esa decisión? Sin estas respuestas claras, cualquier inversión en infraestructura o analítica es solo ruido caro. 👉 Desliza en el carrusel para ver la diferencia exacta entre los datos que generan ROI y los que solo generan costos en la nube, y los pasos clave para empezar a ordenar tu casa digital. 📩 ¿Quieres auditar cómo están tus datos hoy y dónde está la oportunidad real de optimización en tu negocio? Escríbeme un DM y lo revisamos en una sesión breve. dataanalytics dataengineering bussiness bussinesinteligence analytics

Content: Two months in. Here is what building with AI actually looks like for one of our engineers. The technical work was real from week one. A Snowflake MCP Gateway deployed on Azure. A product query agent for a retail client where the debugging never really stopped. An order processing pipeline with human review queues, business rule validation, and a conversational interface operators could use in plain language. Then came the work she did not expect. Co-facilitating AI training sessions for enterprise teams. Building a product-specific agent. Now moving into a finance agent where the data is more sensitive, the rules are stricter, and the margin for error is smaller. The lesson that stuck. The model is almost never where it breaks. It breaks at the context. The schema. The structure of what you hand the system before it is asked to do anything. Pratiksha came in from a marketing analytics background. It turned out to be the thing that made her useful fastest. Knowing what a business team actually needs from a tool is not a soft skill in this work. It is the job. Building a system well and explaining it well are completely different skills. Follow to hear more such stories. Topics: AI engineering, production AI, AI agents, enterprise AI

Content: Trust is usually treated like a brand issue, but in modern marketing it is also tied closely to how well the business is organized behind the scenes. A company can have a good logo, a polished website, and strong visuals, but still create a weak customer experience if the systems behind the marketing are disconnected. You see this when an ad sends people to a page that does not match the offer, when an inquiry form does not trigger the right follow-up, when customer information sits in the wrong place, or when the sales conversation feels disconnected from what the marketing promised. Customers may not see the system behind the business, but they feel the result of it. They feel it when the response is slow, when the next step is unclear, when the message changes from one place to another, or when the business does not feel as organized after the inquiry as it did before. That is why marketing infrastructure matters. The website, CRM, email follow-up, lead handling, advertising, content, automation, and reporting should work together as one connected customer journey. When those pieces are aligned, the business has more control over what happens after someone shows interest. This is where modern marketing systems become valuable. They do not just help a business move faster. They help the business respond more consistently, manage leads more properly, and create a stronger experience from first impression to follow-up. For businesses that want to grow, the goal is not just to generate more attention. The goal is to make sure the system behind the attention is strong enough to support the customer when they are ready to move forward. Graphic direction: Create a LOGIC Marketing Systems static post with the headline “Trust Needs Infrastructure.” Use a modern connected-system visual showing Website, CRM, Email, Ads, Lead Follow-Up, and Reporting linked into one controlled customer journey. Keep the design clean, technical, premium, and business-friendly, with a polished systems architecture feel. Content: Digital Marketing (SEO, PPC & Lead Generation) A business owner told me last year that digital marketing does not work for his industry. I asked him what he had tried. He ran a few Facebook ads two years ago. Got some clicks. No conversions. Stopped. That was his entire data set. One short experiment, written off as proof. Meanwhile his direct competitor, same city, same industry, had spent eighteen months building a content and SEO strategy that now brings in 60% of their revenue from inbound leads. They did not get lucky. They got consistent. Here is what the data actually says about businesses that invest properly in digital marketing. SEO leads have a 14.6% close rate. Outbound leads average 1.7%. The same sales effort closes at nearly nine times the rate when the prospect found you first instead of the other way around. 70% of B2B buyers have already completed their research before they ever contact a vendor. If your business is not visible during that research phase, you do not lose the deal. You never enter the consideration set at all. Businesses that maintain a consistent content strategy generate 67% more leads per month than those that do not. Not from increasing ad spend. From expertise that works while they sleep. The businesses I see winning are rarely the ones with the single best product in their category. They are the ones prospects find first, trust fastest, and remember longest. Marketing is not a cost center sitting separately from the business. It is the mechanism that makes every other part of the business possible to sell. The founder who gave up after one failed ad campaign was not wrong that his first attempt did not work. He was wrong to conclude that meant the channel did not work. Content: Ich glaube, wir haben vergessen, dass Reichweite nicht nur ein Privileg ist. Sondern Verantwortung. In einer Zeit, in der wir mit einem einzigen Post Millionen Menschen erreichen können, reicht es für mich nicht mehr, nur schön auszusehen, schön zu wohnen, Produkte zu verkaufen oder dem nächsten Trend hinterherzulaufen. Denn jede Empfehlung ist eine Entscheidung. Jede Kooperation ist eine Entscheidung. Jedes Schweigen ist eine Entscheidung. Und ich glaube, wir dürfen uns öfter fragen: Würde ich diese Marke auch unterstützen, wenn niemand dafür bezahlen würde? Passt das zu meinen Werten? Mache ich die Welt damit ein kleines bisschen besser oder einfach nur lauter? Für mich bedeutet Social Media zu machen nicht, Menschen zum Kaufen zu bringen. Es bedeutet: Menschen zum Denken zu bringen. Zum Fühlen. Zum Hinterfragen. Zum Verbinden. Ich möchte keine Marken unterstützen, die Menschen ausbeuten. Ich möchte keine Kampagnen machen, für die ich meine Werte an der Garderobe abgebe. Ich möchte meine Stimme nutzen für mentale Gesundheit, Gleichberechtigung, Nachhaltigkeit, Inklusion und die Themen, die mir wirklich wichtig sind. Und nein, ich mache das nicht perfekt. Aber ich versuche es bewusst. Denn wir leben in einer Welt, die uns permanent sagt: Mehr kaufen. Mehr besitzen. Mehr optimieren. Während wir eigentlich viel mehr brauchen: Mehr Menschlichkeit. Mehr Gemeinschaft. Mehr Verantwortung. Vielleicht ist das die eigentliche Frage: Nicht, wie groß unsere Reichweite ist. Sondern wofür wir sie nutzen. 🤍 contentcreator influencermarketing mentalhealth nachhaltigkeit feminismus

Content: Being an employee was never really my thing. For years I’ve been trying to build something of my own, different attempts, different paths, different failures and successes. But things don’t always go the way we want. So I reached a point where I had to start a job again. I like the experience it brings, but the conditions? Not working for me at all. Shifts during weekends for a person like me who believes in work-life balance, who is living away from her hometown and is genuinely alive through her connections and friends, losing the chance of seeing any human being in person during a whole week was far away from my thoughts and expectations. Also the salary sucks and there is no perk and leave at all! But this is the fact of my life right now. I am not complaining. I am sharing this because I know I am not alone in this. Many people, especially founders, are living this double life. Building something meaningful on the side while paying their bills somewhere else. So don’t feel bad or embarrassed if you are experiencing the same, and don’t be surprised when you see a founder with an “Open to Work” badge on their LinkedIn profile. That’s not failure. That’s just the part nobody posts about. I chose this path consciously. Not because I had no options, but because building something meaningful takes time, and time needs stability. The job is the bridge. What I’m building is the destination. If you’re somewhere in this same messy in-between, building on the side, figuring it out, holding two lives at once, my DM is open. You don’t have to figure it out alone. solofounder solopreneur enterpreneur startup

Content: Viele denken, ein guter Prompt braucht nur klare Fragen. Doch oft sind es genau diese drei Fehler, die dich stundenlang Daten nachbearbeiten lassen. 1. Zu viele Details auf einmal. Die KI verliert den Fokus und liefert unbrauchbare Antworten. Besser: Schritt für Schritt vorgehen. Ein Beispiel: Statt „Schreib mir einen Blogpost über KI im Marketing mit Beispielen, Struktur und Call-to-Action“ erst den Titel klären, dann die Gliederung, dann die Beispiele. 2. Kein Kontext für die KI. Ohne Hintergrundwissen produziert sie generische Texte. Sag ihr, für wen du schreibst und welchen Ton du brauchst. „Schreib für Solopreneure, die wenig Zeit haben. Kurz, konkret, ohne Fachchinesisch.“ 3. Keine Beispiele vorgeben. Die KI orientiert sich an deinen Vorgaben. Gib ihr ein Muster, wie die Antwort aussehen soll. „Antworte in kurzen Absätzen, maximal drei Sätze pro Punkt.“ Probier’s aus: Nimm einen alten Prompt und streiche alles, was nicht direkt zur Aufgabe gehört. Du wirst sehen, die Antworten werden präziser. Content: Codex just changed what "programming" means for most people. OpenAI shipped Record & Replay in Codex: you perform a task while Codex watches, and it generates a SKILL.md — a human-readable instruction file that any agent can execute. What comes out the other side is a shareable, editable workflow document, readable by humans and LLMs alike. One employee records a process. The whole team runs it as automation. This is what makes the "no-code agent" promise real. The workflow documentation and the automation are the same artifact. Show it once, and it runs. For enterprise teams watching the RPA space: this pattern competes directly with UiPath and Automation Anywhere on use cases where automation needs to generalize across context, not just replay steps blindly. Content: Automation feels powerful until it starts protecting the wrong thing. That is the part people do not talk about enough. You build AI workflows to reduce friction. You expect them to remember the rhythm, protect the important routines, and keep the company moving while you focus on higher-level decisions. But when pressure spikes, a system without judgment can become loud in the wrong direction. It can treat a trusted daily publishing pipeline like a disposable experiment. It can pause continuity while preserving noise. It can quietly break the public trail, memory refresh, product maintenance, or recovery loop that made the system trustworthy in the first place. The fix was not just “run less automation.” The fix was clearer operating judgment: • protect core routines first • pause optional experiments before trusted pipelines • preserve memory and publishing rhythm • keep receipts when something changes • separate pressure from priority This is what I mean when I say AI agents need operating systems, not just prompts. I am building Lucy around that layer in public. If you are building with AI agents, automation, workflows, digital products, or autonomous systems, save this. This is the boring part that makes the exciting part trustworthy. Content: Remote Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers just changed what AI agents can actually do. Until now, MCP only ran locally — on your machine, for developers, behind a terminal. Cool for coders. Useless for everyone else. Remote MCP servers flip that completely. Sign in, grant permissions, done. Your AI agent can now send emails, deploy code, book things, and pull from real accounts — all through a secure OAuth flow that actually works 🔒 Think about what that means. It's basically the jump from desktop software to the web; nobody installs apps anymore, and nobody should have to install MCP servers either. That shift felt inevitable in hindsight, and this one will too. Cloudflare just made this deployable in under two minutes. The auth layer, the token handling, the transport — handled. You just build the tools your agent needs. This matters more than most teams realize right now. What does this unlock? Consumer-facing AI that connects to real-world services without a single line of setup from the end user. That's not a developer toy anymore. That's a platform shift ⚡ Save this if you're building AI agents and haven't looked at remote MCP yet; you're going to want to. What tool or service would you want your AI agent to access first? Drop it below 👇 modelcontextprotocol mcpservers aiagents cloudflaredevelopers llmtools
